title: The QP's Role for Investigational Medicinal Products (IMPs)
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How QP IMP certification works under EU GMP Annex 13 and MHRA rules: the release decision, importation, comparators, labelling and the risks inspectors probe.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette layered over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent glow. Soft natural light falls across a pristine clinical-supply packaging bench where gloved hands rest beside a sealed, blinded investigational medicine carton and a row of softly blurred clinical-trial labelled vials and a numbered subject kit out of focus in the background; a sleek tablet shows abstract batch-record data. Shallow depth of field draws the eye to the precise labelling and the considered release gesture. Cinematic, premium, sterile yet warm, immaculate surfaces with gentle reflections, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What does a QP certify an IMP batch against if there is no marketing authorisation?
faqs.a: For investigational medicinal products the reference point is the product specification file and the clinical trial authorisation, not a marketing authorisation. The QP confirms that the batch was manufactured and checked in line with the dossier submitted to the competent authority and the arrangements described in it. This is governed by EU GMP Annex 13 and certified under the principles of Annex 16, with the equivalent UK framework enforced by the MHRA.

faqs.q: Is full importation testing always required for IMPs made in a third country?
faqs.a: No. Unlike commercial product, the requirement for full re-testing of imported IMPs can be reduced or waived where the QP holds documented assurance of the manufacturing and testing arrangements, supported by the product specification file. That assurance must rest on genuine supplier qualification, a clear quality agreement and verified oversight of the overseas site, set proportionately to risk under ICH Q9. It is never a default position and assuming equivalence without evidence invites a critical finding.

faqs.q: How does the QP handle comparator products in a blinded clinical trial?
faqs.a: When a licensed product is sourced as a comparator and re-packaged or re-labelled for blinding, the QP must be satisfied that the original quality has been preserved and that the manipulation has not compromised the product. The provenance chain from authorised supply through to clinical site must be intact and documented. Annex 13 also sets specific clinical-trial labelling requirements that the certified batch must meet.
